instruction: Implement end-to-end type safety using tRPC in a TypeScript full-stack application. Show how to define type-safe procedures, handle errors, and share types between server and client without code generation.
output:
<think>
tRPC eliminates the API contract gap between frontend and backend by sharing TypeScript types directly. Unlike REST+OpenAPI or GraphQL, there's no code generation step — the types flow naturally through TypeScript's type inference.

The architecture: define procedures on the server with input/output schemas (using Zod for runtime validation), create a router, then create a typed client that infers all types from the router definition. The client gets full autocomplete, type checking, and error handling without any generated code.

Key components:
1. Server: define procedures with Zod input validation, organize in routers
2. Shared: the AppRouter type is exported and imported by the client
3. Client: createTRPCProxyClient infers all types from AppRouter
4. Error handling: type-safe error codes, custom error formatters

The magic is in the type inference chain: Zod schema -> inferred TypeScript type -> procedure type -> router type -> client type. Everything is connected through TypeScript's type system.

I'll show a complete example with user CRUD operations, authentication middleware, and error handling.
</think>

```typescript
// === server/trpc.ts — tRPC initialization ===

import { initTRPC, TRPCError } from '@trpc/server';
import { z } from 'zod';

// Context type — available in all procedures
interface Context {
  userId: string | null;
  db: {
    users: Map<string, { id: string; name: string; email: string; role: string }>;
  };
}

const t = initTRPC.context<Context>().create({
  errorFormatter({ shape, error }) {
    return {
      ...shape,
      data: {
        ...shape.data,
        // Add custom error fields
        zodError:
          error.code === 'BAD_REQUEST' && error.cause instanceof z.ZodError
            ? error.cause.flatten()
            : null,
      },
    };
  },
});

export const router = t.router;
export const publicProcedure = t.procedure;

// Auth middleware — reusable across procedures
const isAuthenticated = t.middleware(({ ctx, next }) => {
  if (!ctx.userId) {
    throw new TRPCError({
      code: 'UNAUTHORIZED',
      message: 'You must be logged in',
    });
  }
  return next({
    ctx: {
      ...ctx,
      userId: ctx.userId, // Now non-null in downstream procedures
    },
  });
});

export const protectedProcedure = t.procedure.use(isAuthenticated);


// === server/routers/user.ts — User router ===

// Zod schemas define BOTH runtime validation AND TypeScript types
const createUserSchema = z.object({
  name: z.string().min(1).max(100),
  email: z.string().email(),
  role: z.enum(['user', 'admin']).default('user'),
});

const updateUserSchema = z.object({
  id: z.string().uuid(),
  name: z.string().min(1).max(100).optional(),
  email: z.string().email().optional(),
});

export const userRouter = router({
  // Query: get user by ID (public)
  getById: publicProcedure
    .input(z.object({ id: z.string().uuid() }))
    .query(({ input, ctx }) => {
      const user = ctx.db.users.get(input.id);
      if (!user) {
        throw new TRPCError({
          code: 'NOT_FOUND',
          message: `User ${input.id} not found`,
        });
      }
      return user; // Return type is inferred!
    }),

  // Query: list all users (public)
  list: publicProcedure
    .input(
      z.object({
        limit: z.number().min(1).max(100).default(20),
        cursor: z.string().optional(),
      })
    )
    .query(({ input, ctx }) => {
      const users = Array.from(ctx.db.users.values());
      return {
        users: users.slice(0, input.limit),
        nextCursor: users.length > input.limit ? users[input.limit].id : null,
      };
    }),

  // Mutation: create user (protected)
  create: protectedProcedure
    .input(createUserSchema)
    .mutation(({ input, ctx }) => {
      const id = crypto.randomUUID();
      const user = { id, ...input };
      ctx.db.users.set(id, user);
      return user;
    }),

  // Mutation: update user (protected)
  update: protectedProcedure
    .input(updateUserSchema)
    .mutation(({ input, ctx }) => {
      const existing = ctx.db.users.get(input.id);
      if (!existing) {
        throw new TRPCError({ code: 'NOT_FOUND' });
      }
      const updated = { ...existing, ...input };
      ctx.db.users.set(input.id, updated);
      return updated;
    }),

  // Mutation: delete user (protected, admin only)
  delete: protectedProcedure
    .input(z.object({ id: z.string().uuid() }))
    .mutation(({ input, ctx }) => {
      const user = ctx.db.users.get(ctx.userId);
      if (user?.role !== 'admin') {
        throw new TRPCError({
          code: 'FORBIDDEN',
          message: 'Admin access required',
        });
      }
      ctx.db.users.delete(input.id);
      return { success: true };
    }),
});


// === server/root.ts — Root router ===

const appRouter = router({
  user: userRouter,
});

// THIS is the type that bridges server and client
// No code generation needed — just a TypeScript type export
export type AppRouter = typeof appRouter;


// === client/trpc.ts — Type-safe client ===

import { createTRPCProxyClient, httpBatchLink, TRPCClientError } from '@trpc/client';
import type { AppRouter } from '../server/root';
// Only importing the TYPE — no server code in the client bundle

const trpc = createTRPCProxyClient<AppRouter>({
  links: [
    httpBatchLink({
      url: 'http://localhost:3000/trpc',
      headers() {
        return {
          authorization: `Bearer ${getToken()}`,
        };
      },
    }),
  ],
});

function getToken(): string {
  return localStorage.getItem('token') ?? '';
}

// === Client usage — fully type-safe! ===

async function demo() {
  // TypeScript knows the return type: { id, name, email, role }
  const user = await trpc.user.getById.query({ id: 'some-uuid' });
  console.log(user.name); // autocomplete works!

  // TypeScript validates the input shape at compile time
  const newUser = await trpc.user.create.mutate({
    name: 'Alice',
    email: 'alice@example.com',
    // role is optional with default 'user'
  });

  // Pagination with cursor
  const page = await trpc.user.list.query({ limit: 10 });
  console.log(page.users, page.nextCursor);

  // Type-safe error handling
  try {
    await trpc.user.delete.mutate({ id: 'some-uuid' });
  } catch (error) {
    if (error instanceof TRPCClientError) {
      // error.data.code is typed: 'UNAUTHORIZED' | 'FORBIDDEN' | etc.
      if (error.data?.code === 'FORBIDDEN') {
        console.error('Need admin access');
      }
      // Zod validation errors are structured
      if (error.data?.zodError) {
        console.error('Validation:', error.data.zodError.fieldErrors);
      }
    }
  }
}
```

instruction: Implement Zod schema validation for a TypeScript API with custom error messages, transform pipelines, discriminated unions, and recursive types. Show how to derive TypeScript types from schemas and use them across the application.
output:
<think>
Zod is the standard for runtime type validation in TypeScript. It bridges the gap between runtime data (JSON from APIs, form inputs) and compile-time types. The key feature: you define the schema once and derive the TypeScript type from it, ensuring they can never drift apart.

Key features to cover:
1. Basic schemas with custom error messages
2. Transform pipelines (coerce string to number, trim strings, etc.)
3. Discriminated unions (tagged unions for polymorphic data)
4. Recursive types (tree structures, nested comments)
5. Type derivation with z.infer<>
6. Refinements (custom validation logic)
7. Composition (merge, extend, pick, omit)

Transform pipelines are powerful: they let you validate AND transform data in one step. For example, parse a date string into a Date object, or coerce a query parameter string to a number.

Discriminated unions with z.discriminatedUnion use a literal field to determine which variant to parse. This is more efficient than z.union (which tries each variant sequentially) and gives better error messages.

Recursive types require z.lazy() because TypeScript/Zod can't handle circular references directly. The pattern is: define the base schema, then use z.lazy(() => schema) for the recursive part.
</think>

```typescript
import { z } from 'zod';

// === Basic schemas with custom errors ===

const emailSchema = z
  .string({ required_error: 'Email is required' })
  .email({ message: 'Invalid email format' })
  .toLowerCase()
  .trim();

const passwordSchema = z
  .string()
  .min(8, 'Password must be at least 8 characters')
  .max(128, 'Password too long')
  .regex(/[A-Z]/, 'Must contain an uppercase letter')
  .regex(/[0-9]/, 'Must contain a number')
  .regex(/[^A-Za-z0-9]/, 'Must contain a special character');


// === Transform pipelines ===

// Coerce string query params to proper types
const paginationSchema = z.object({
  page: z.coerce.number().int().positive().default(1),
  limit: z.coerce.number().int().min(1).max(100).default(20),
  sort: z
    .string()
    .transform((val) => {
      const [field, order] = val.split(':');
      return { field, order: (order as 'asc' | 'desc') || 'asc' };
    })
    .optional(),
});

// Parse ISO string to Date with validation
const dateRangeSchema = z.object({
  start: z.string().datetime().transform((s) => new Date(s)),
  end: z.string().datetime().transform((s) => new Date(s)),
}).refine(
  (data) => data.start < data.end,
  { message: 'Start date must be before end date', path: ['start'] }
);


// === Discriminated unions (tagged unions) ===

const notificationSchema = z.discriminatedUnion('type', [
  z.object({
    type: z.literal('email'),
    to: z.string().email(),
    subject: z.string(),
    body: z.string(),
  }),
  z.object({
    type: z.literal('sms'),
    phone: z.string().regex(/^\+[1-9]\d{1,14}$/, 'Invalid phone number'),
    message: z.string().max(160),
  }),
  z.object({
    type: z.literal('push'),
    deviceToken: z.string(),
    title: z.string(),
    body: z.string(),
    data: z.record(z.string()).optional(),
  }),
]);


// === Recursive types ===

interface Comment {
  id: string;
  text: string;
  author: string;
  replies: Comment[];
  createdAt: Date;
}

const commentSchema: z.ZodType<Comment> = z.lazy(() =>
  z.object({
    id: z.string().uuid(),
    text: z.string().min(1).max(10000),
    author: z.string(),
    replies: z.array(commentSchema).default([]),
    createdAt: z.coerce.date(),
  })
);

// Tree structure
interface TreeNode {
  value: string;
  children: TreeNode[];
}

const treeSchema: z.ZodType<TreeNode> = z.lazy(() =>
  z.object({
    value: z.string(),
    children: z.array(treeSchema).default([]),
  })
);


// === Schema composition ===

const baseUserSchema = z.object({
  name: z.string().min(1).max(100),
  email: emailSchema,
  role: z.enum(['user', 'admin', 'moderator']),
});

// Extend for creation (adds password)
const createUserSchema = baseUserSchema.extend({
  password: passwordSchema,
});

// Partial for updates (all fields optional)
const updateUserSchema = baseUserSchema.partial().extend({
  id: z.string().uuid(),
});

// Pick specific fields
const userProfileSchema = baseUserSchema.pick({
  name: true,
  email: true,
});


// === Type derivation — types always match schemas ===

type Email = z.infer<typeof emailSchema>;
type Pagination = z.infer<typeof paginationSchema>;
type DateRange = z.infer<typeof dateRangeSchema>;
type Notification = z.infer<typeof notificationSchema>;
type CreateUser = z.infer<typeof createUserSchema>;
type UpdateUser = z.infer<typeof updateUserSchema>;
type UserProfile = z.infer<typeof userProfileSchema>;


// === Usage with safe parsing ===

function handleRequest(rawInput: unknown): void {
  // safeParse doesn't throw — returns success/error discriminated union
  const result = createUserSchema.safeParse(rawInput);

  if (!result.success) {
    // Structured error with paths and messages
    const formatted = result.error.format();
    console.error('Validation errors:', formatted);
    // formatted.email?._errors -> ["Invalid email format"]
    // formatted.password?._errors -> ["Must contain a number"]
    return;
  }

  // result.data is fully typed as CreateUser
  const user: CreateUser = result.data;
  console.log(`Creating user: ${user.name} <${user.email}>`);
}


// === Demo ===

// Valid input
handleRequest({
  name: 'Alice',
  email: '  ALICE@Example.COM  ',  // will be trimmed + lowercased
  password: 'MyP@ssw0rd!',
  role: 'user',
});

// Transform pipeline demo
const pagination = paginationSchema.parse({
  page: '3',     // string coerced to number
  limit: '50',
  sort: 'name:desc',  // transformed to { field, order }
});
console.log('Pagination:', pagination);
// { page: 3, limit: 50, sort: { field: 'name', order: 'desc' } }

// Discriminated union demo
const notification = notificationSchema.parse({
  type: 'sms',
  phone: '+1234567890',
  message: 'Hello!',
});
console.log('Notification type:', notification.type);
```

instruction: Implement type-safe server components and routing in a Next.js/React application using TypeScript. Show how to handle data fetching with proper error boundaries, loading states, and type-safe route parameters.
output:
Server components in Next.js (App Router) represent a paradigm shift: components run on the server by default, can directly access databases and APIs, and send only the rendered HTML to the client. Type safety across this boundary is crucial. Key areas: 1. Type-safe route parameters: use TypeScript to enforce that route params match expected shapes 2. Server component data fetching: async components that fetch data directly 3. Error boundaries: typed error handling at route segment level 4. Loading states: Suspense-based loading UI 5. Server actions: type-safe form mutations 6. Shared types between server and client components The App Router uses file-system based routing where [slug] creates dynamic segments. We can type these params and searchParams to catch errors at compile time. Server components can be async (await directly in the component body), which simplifies data fetching enormously compared to useEffect. But we need proper error boundaries and loading states. I'll show a complete example with a blog post page: typed route params, server-side data fetching, error boundary, loading state, and a server action for comments. ```typescript // === types/blog.ts — Shared types === export interface BlogPost { id: string; slug: string; title: string; content: string; author: { id: string; name: string; avatar: string }; publishedAt: Date; tags: string[]; } export interface Comment { id: string; postId: string; author: string; text: string; createdAt: Date; } // === app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx — Type-safe server component === import { notFound } from 'next/navigation'; import { Suspense } from 'react'; import type { BlogPost, Comment } from '@/types/blog'; // Type-safe route params interface BlogPostPageProps { params: { slug: string }; searchParams: { highlight?: string }; } // Server-side data fetching functions async function getPost(slug: string): Promise { const res = await fetch(`${process.env.API_URL}/posts/${slug}`, { next: { revalidate: 60 }, // ISR: revalidate every 60s }); if (res.status === 404) return null; if (!res.ok) throw new Error(`Failed to fetch post: ${res.status}`); return res.json(); } async function getComments(postId: string): Promise { const res = await fetch(`${process.env.API_URL}/posts/${postId}/comments`, { cache: 'no-store', // Always fresh comments }); if (!res.ok) throw new Error('Failed to fetch comments'); return res.json(); } // Async server component — fetches data directly export default async function BlogPostPage({ params, searchParams }: BlogPostPageProps) { const post = await getPost(params.slug); if (!post) { notFound(); // Triggers not-found.tsx boundary } return (

{post.title}

{post.author.name} {new Date(post.publishedAt).toLocaleDateString()}
{post.tags.map((tag) => ( {tag} ))}
{/* Suspense boundary: comments load independently */}

Comments

}>
{/* Server action form */}
); } // Async sub-component (server) — streams in after main content async function CommentsSection({ postId }: { postId: string }) { const comments = await getComments(postId); if (comments.length === 0) { return

No comments yet.

; } return (
    {comments.map((comment) => (

  • {comment.author}

    {comment.text}

    {new Date(comment.createdAt).toLocaleDateString()}
    • ))}
); } function CommentsSkeleton() { return (
{[1, 2, 3].map((i) => (
))}
); } // === app/blog/[slug]/actions.ts — Type-safe server action === 'use server'; import { z } from 'zod'; import { revalidatePath } from 'next/cache'; const commentSchema = z.object({ postId: z.string().uuid(), author: z.string().min(1).max(100), text: z.string().min(1).max(5000), }); type ActionResult = { success: true } | { success: false; error: string }; export async function addComment(formData: FormData): Promise { const raw = { postId: formData.get('postId'), author: formData.get('author'), text: formData.get('text'), }; const result = commentSchema.safeParse(raw); if (!result.success) { return { success: false, error: result.error.issues[0].message }; } const res = await fetch(`${process.env.API_URL}/comments`, { method: 'POST', headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' }, body: JSON.stringify(result.data), }); if (!res.ok) { return { success: false, error: 'Failed to add comment' }; } revalidatePath(`/blog/${result.data.postId}`); return { success: true }; } // === Client component for the form === 'use client'; import { useFormState, useFormStatus } from 'react-dom'; import { addComment } from './actions'; function AddCommentForm({ postId }: { postId: string }) { const [state, formAction] = useFormState(addComment, null); return (

instruction: Implement type-safe routing in a TypeScript application where route parameters, query strings, and route paths are all validated at compile time. Show how to prevent invalid navigation and extract typed params from URLs.
output:
<think>
Type-safe routing means the TypeScript compiler catches navigation errors: linking to non-existent routes, missing required params, wrong param types. This eliminates an entire class of runtime 404 errors.

The approach: define routes as a const object with template string types. Use TypeScript template literal types to extract param names from route patterns like '/users/:id/posts/:postId'. Then create utility types and functions that enforce correct params at call sites.

Key techniques:
1. Template literal types to parse route patterns: '/users/:id' -> { id: string }
2. Const assertion to preserve literal types in the route definition
3. Generic helper function that enforces correct params based on the route pattern
4. Type-safe Link component that only allows valid routes and params
5. Type-safe useParams hook that returns correctly typed params

The template literal type parsing is the most advanced part. We recursively split the route pattern on '/' and collect segments that start with ':' as parameter names. TypeScript 4.1+ template literal types make this possible.

I'll also handle query string typing: each route can declare its accepted query parameters with their types.
</think>

```typescript
// === Route definition with const assertion ===

const routes = {
  home: '/',
  users: '/users',
  userDetail: '/users/:userId',
  userPosts: '/users/:userId/posts',
  postDetail: '/users/:userId/posts/:postId',
  settings: '/settings/:section',
} as const;

type Routes = typeof routes;
type RouteName = keyof Routes;
type RoutePath = Routes[RouteName];


// === Extract params from route pattern using template literal types ===

// Parse ':paramName' segments from a route pattern
type ExtractParams<T extends string> =
  T extends `${infer _Start}:${infer Param}/${infer Rest}`
    ? { [K in Param | keyof ExtractParams<Rest>]: string }
    : T extends `${infer _Start}:${infer Param}`
    ? { [K in Param]: string }
    : {};

// Test the type extraction (compile-time only)
type UserDetailParams = ExtractParams<'/users/:userId'>;
// = { userId: string }

type PostDetailParams = ExtractParams<'/users/:userId/posts/:postId'>;
// = { userId: string; postId: string }

type HomeParams = ExtractParams<'/'>;
// = {}


// === Type-safe URL builder ===

type ParamsFor<R extends RouteName> = ExtractParams<Routes[R]>;

type HasParams<R extends RouteName> =
  keyof ParamsFor<R> extends never ? false : true;

// Build URL from route name and params
function buildUrl<R extends RouteName>(
  ...args: HasParams<R> extends true
    ? [route: R, params: ParamsFor<R>]
    : [route: R]
): string {
  const [routeName, params] = args as [R, Record<string, string>?];
  let path: string = routes[routeName];

  if (params) {
    for (const [key, value] of Object.entries(params)) {
      path = path.replace(`:${key}`, encodeURIComponent(value));
    }
  }

  return path;
}

// === Type-safe query string ===

interface RouteQueryParams {
  users: { page?: number; search?: string; role?: 'user' | 'admin' };
  userPosts: { page?: number; tag?: string };
}

function buildUrlWithQuery<R extends RouteName>(
  route: R,
  params: HasParams<R> extends true ? ParamsFor<R> : undefined,
  query?: R extends keyof RouteQueryParams ? RouteQueryParams[R] : never
): string {
  const base = params
    ? buildUrl(route as any, params as any)
    : buildUrl(route as any);

  if (!query) return base;

  const searchParams = new URLSearchParams();
  for (const [key, value] of Object.entries(query as Record<string, any>)) {
    if (value !== undefined) {
      searchParams.set(key, String(value));
    }
  }

  const qs = searchParams.toString();
  return qs ? `${base}?${qs}` : base;
}


// === Type-safe route matching ===

function matchRoute<R extends RouteName>(
  routeName: R,
  pathname: string
): ParamsFor<R> | null {
  const pattern = routes[routeName];
  const patternParts = pattern.split('/');
  const pathParts = pathname.split('/');

  if (patternParts.length !== pathParts.length) return null;

  const params: Record<string, string> = {};

  for (let i = 0; i < patternParts.length; i++) {
    if (patternParts[i].startsWith(':')) {
      const paramName = patternParts[i].slice(1);
      params[paramName] = decodeURIComponent(pathParts[i]);
    } else if (patternParts[i] !== pathParts[i]) {
      return null;
    }
  }

  return params as ParamsFor<R>;
}


// === Usage examples ===

// Correct usage — all type-checked
const url1 = buildUrl('home');
const url2 = buildUrl('userDetail', { userId: '123' });
const url3 = buildUrl('postDetail', { userId: '123', postId: '456' });

// These would cause COMPILE ERRORS:
// buildUrl('userDetail');  // Error: missing params
// buildUrl('userDetail', { id: '123' }); // Error: wrong param name
// buildUrl('nonexistent', {}); // Error: route doesn't exist
// buildUrl('home', { id: '1' }); // Error: home has no params

// Route matching
const params = matchRoute('postDetail', '/users/123/posts/456');
if (params) {
  // params is typed as { userId: string; postId: string }
  console.log(`User: ${params.userId}, Post: ${params.postId}`);
}

// Query string
const url4 = buildUrlWithQuery('users', undefined, { page: 2, role: 'admin' });
console.log(url4); // /users?page=2&role=admin

console.log('Built URLs:', { url1, url2, url3, url4 });
console.log('All type-safe routing checks PASS');
```
